postData.contentHtml:
A new era of economic partnership has dawned between India and New Zealand with the signing of a comprehensive free trade agreement (FTA). Hailed as a "once-in-a-generation" pact, this landmark deal is set to significantly deepen economic ties and diversify trade for both countries.

After nine months of intensive negotiations, the agreement will dramatically cut or eliminate tariffs on a broad spectrum of goods. A remarkable 95% of New Zealand's exports to India, encompassing crucial products like seafood, iron, steel, and scrap aluminum, will benefit from reduced or eliminated tariffs. Notably, tariffs on key fruit imports such as kiwifruit and apples are also slated for reduction. Over half of New Zealand's exports to India will immediately become duty-free, with the remaining items phased in over time.

Beyond the exchange of goods, the pact introduces substantial provisions for facilitating movement and investment. It allocates 5,000 temporary employment visas for Indian professionals and 1,000 working holiday visas, alongside easing post-study work rights for Indian students. In a significant commitment, New Zealand has pledged a $20 billion investment. India, in turn, will open market access across 118 services sectors, including professional, audio-visual, computer-related, construction, telecommunications, and tourism.

While the agreement is largely seen as a positive step, India has strategically maintained protection for its sensitive agricultural sectors, such as dairy, coffee, sugar, spices, edible oils, and rubber. This particular stance, aimed at safeguarding domestic producers, has been a point of disappointment for New Zealand's prominent dairy industry.

The future looks bright for bilateral trade, with projections indicating a doubling to approximately $5 billion over the next five years. New Zealand has an ambitious goal of doubling its exports within a decade. The agreement is poised to provide a significant boost to key Indian export sectors, including textiles, leather, pharmaceuticals, engineering goods, and automobiles. Additionally, it will grant India duty-free access to essential industrial inputs.

Enjoying bipartisan support in New Zealand, the agreement is widely anticipated to pass parliamentary approval. Currently, two-way merchandise trade stood at around $1.3 billion in 2024/25, with total goods and services trade estimated at $2.4 billion in 2024. Social sentiment surrounding the trade pact is generally positive, albeit with some ongoing discussions regarding its specifics.

This comprehensive trade agreement marks a pivotal moment, promising to unlock new opportunities and foster stronger economic collaboration between India and New Zealand for years to come.
postData.title: India & New Zealand Forge 'Once-in-a-Generation' Trade Alliance
postData.summary: India and New Zealand have signed a landmark free trade agreement, set to slash tariffs on a wide range of goods including fruits, boost exports, and ease visa access, aiming to double bilateral trade to $5 billion within five years. This 'once-in-a-generation' pact promises deeper economic ties and diversified trade for both nations.
